ontology.foodex2_description: Mushrooms, canned — Agaricus bisporus preserved in brine or water
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for canned button mushroom as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage for processed vegetable products is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of canned button mushrooms is approximately 85 g drained weight, equivalent to roughly half a standard 185 g drained can, commonly used as a side dish or ingredient in cooked meals.

nutrient_source.title: Canned mushrooms — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for canned button mushroom (F14030002). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Vegetables and Fungi — Analogous estimate based on mushrooms (Agaricus bisporus, cooked/canned); GI of mushrooms reported as approximately 10–15 in peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for canned button mushroom in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 15 based on the closest analogous entry for cooked Agaricus bisporus mushrooms, which are consistently reported in the low GI range (≤15) due to negligible digestible starch content. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 6.375) / 100 ≈ 1.0 (serving size 85 g, carbohydrate 7.5 g/100 g → 6.375 g per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from brine preservation (315 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Canned button mushrooms contain 315 mg sodium per 100 g due to brine preservation; patients with hypertension should drain and rinse thoroughly before use, which can reduce sodium by up to 40%, and should prefer fresh or frozen mushrooms as a lower-sodium alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (7.5 g/100 g), very low sugar (0.6 g/100 g), and low estimated GI (~15)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Canned button mushrooms have a low glycaemic index (~15) and minimal digestible carbohydrate, making them a suitable vegetable choice for individuals with type 2 diabetes; rinsing before use is advised to reduce sodium, which at high intake may indirectly affect insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat and cholesterol, but elevated sodium (315 mg/100 g) is a cardiovascular risk factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Canned button mushrooms are virtually fat-free and cholesterol-free, which is favourable for cardiovascular health, but the elevated sodium content (315 mg/100 g) warrants caution; draining and rinsing before consumption is recommended to reduce sodium load for individuals managing cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beta-glucan content with immune-modulating properties; negligible fat and no trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Button mushrooms contain beta-glucans that may modulate immune response relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; however, the elevated sodium from canning (315 mg/100 g) may contribute to tissue swelling, so thorough rinsing before use is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Canned button mushroom (Agaricus bisporus) is a commercially preserved vegetable product consisting of whole or sliced white mushrooms packed in brine or water and heat-sterilised for shelf stability. The canning process imparts a softer texture compared to fresh mushrooms and introduces significant sodium (315 mg/100 g) from the brine. The flavour is mild, earthy, and slightly saline. Nutritionally, canned button mushrooms are very low in energy (33 kcal/100 g), negligible in fat (0 g/100 g — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo; fat is naturally absent in this fungal product), and low in protein (0.7 g/100 g). Carbohydrate is 7.5 g/100 g with very low sugar (0.6 g/100 g) and modest dietary fibre (0.7 g/100 g). The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), making this food suitable for blood glucose management when sodium is controlled. The primary clinical concern is the elevated sodium content, which is relevant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Draining and rinsing canned mushrooms before use can reduce sodium by up to 40%. Canned button mushrooms are widely used in global cuisines as a convenient ingredient in stir-fries, pasta sauces, soups, and casseroles. They are vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and halal-suitable. Beta-glucans present in button mushrooms have immune-modulating properties of relevance to inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in canned button mushrooms?
Are canned mushrooms suitable for hypertension?
How to reduce sodium in canned mushrooms?
Canned mushroom vs fresh mushroom nutrition

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo (F14030002) with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked Agaricus bisporus, clearly documented. All mandatory fields are complete.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo (F14030002); fat is naturally absent in this fungal product with no added fat in the canning process. saturated_fat_g and cholesterol_mg are confirmed zero by product formulation. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero — no hydrogenated oils are used in canning. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry. potassium_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo source entry; estimated at approximately 150 mg/100 g in secondary literature but not confirmed by an approved primary source and therefore not populated. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail beyond calcium and iron not reported in HPB Foodo for this canned mushroom entry. GI value of 15 is an analogous estimate based on cooked Agaricus bisporus from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct canned button mushroom GI entry exists in the database. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; it is a generic internationally traded canned product, so no mandatory local-language names apply under G6 multilingual rules.
